verses.commentary: Tax collectors and sinners were drawing near to hear him. Sinful crowd recurring around Jesus.

Pharisees and scribes grumbled: 'This man receives sinners and eats with them.' The grumble (diegongguzon, διεγόγγυζον — emphatic complaining) is the parables' occasion. Three parables answer the grumble.
He told them this parable. Singular 'parable' covers all three — they are one unit.
'What man of you, having a hundred sheep and losing one, does not leave the ninety-nine in the open country and go after the lost one until he finds it?' Search until found.
Finding it, lays it on his shoulders rejoicing. Pastoral imagery — the recovery carrying.
Calls friends and neighbors: 'Rejoice with me, I have found my lost sheep.' Communal celebration of recovery.

'There will be more joy in heaven over one sinner who repents than over ninety-nine righteous persons who need no repentance.' The 'righteous' here is ironic — applied to those who think they need no repentance.
Or what woman, having ten silver coins, if she loses one — lights a lamp, sweeps the house, seeks diligently until she finds it? Female protagonist, domestic setting.
When she finds it — calls friends and neighbors: 'Rejoice with me, I have found my lost coin.' Same celebratory pattern.
'There is joy before the angels of God over one sinner who repents.' Heaven's value-system.
There was a man who had two sons. Most beloved parable — the climax of the lost-found triad.
Younger: 'Father, give me the share of property that is coming to me.' Premature inheritance — culturally a death-wish toward the father. He divided his property between them.

Not many days later — gathered all, journeyed to a far country, squandered (dieskorpisen, διεσκόρπισεν) his property in reckless (asotos, ἀσώτως — riotous, prodigal) living. Geographic and moral distance both maximized.
Spent everything — severe famine in that country — began to be in need. Resources exhausted just as crisis arrives.
Hired himself to a citizen of that country — sent him to feed pigs. For a Jew, the lowest possible job (unclean animals). Identity reversal.
Longed to fill himself with the pods (keration, κερατίων — carob pods) that the pigs ate. No one gave him anything. Starvation amid feed for pigs.

When he came to himself (eis heauton elthon, εἰς ἑαυτὸν ἐλθὼν) — significant phrase. Repentance is self-return. 'How many of my father's hired servants have more than enough bread, while I perish here with hunger.'
'I will arise and go to my father, and I will say: Father, I have sinned against heaven and before you.' Drafted confession. Vertical and horizontal sin acknowledged.
'I am no longer worthy to be called your son; treat me as one of your hired servants.' Status forfeited, employment requested.

He arose, came to his father. 'But while he was still a long way off, his father saw him and felt compassion (esplagchnisthe, ἐσπλαγχνίσθη) and ran (dramon, δραμὼν — running was undignified for a Middle Eastern patriarch) and embraced him and kissed him.' Father's preemptive mercy.
Son begins his rehearsed speech — 'Father, I have sinned... no longer worthy to be called your son.' The third clause ('treat me as a servant') is cut off by the father's interruption.

Father to servants: 'Bring the best robe — put it on him. Ring on his hand. Shoes on his feet.' Three signs of sonship — robe (status), ring (authority), shoes (free man, not slave). Total restoration.
'Bring the fattened calf, kill it — let us eat and celebrate.' The kept-for-special-occasion calf. The biggest party.
'This my son was dead and is alive again; he was lost and is found.' Two formulations for the same recovery. Celebration begins.
Older son in the field. As he came near the house — heard music and dancing.
Calls one of the servants — asks what these things mean. Outsider to the celebration.
'Your brother has come, your father killed the fattened calf because he received him back safe and sound.'
Older brother angry — refused to go in. Father came out and entreated him. The same father who ran to the prodigal goes out to the elder.

'These many years I have served (douleuo, δουλεύω — slaved) for you — never disobeyed your command — yet you never gave me a young goat to celebrate with my friends.' Service framed as slavery; resentment exposed.
'But when this son of yours (not 'my brother') came, who has devoured your property with prostitutes, you killed the fattened calf.' Disowns his brother; categorizes the gift as injustice.
'Son (teknon, τέκνον — affectionate), you are always with me, and all that is mine is yours.' The elder has all along had access to everything.

'It was fitting (edei, ἔδει — necessary) to celebrate and be glad — your brother was dead and is alive; was lost and is found.' Open-ended parable. Will the elder brother enter? The Pharisees — who are the parable's elder brothers — must answer for themselves.
